findings.title: No cookie consent banner or cookie consent mechanism
findings.description: The application uses Plausible analytics on the marketing site without a cookie/consent banner to allow users to opt-in or opt-out. GDPR requires explicit, freely given, specific, and informed consent before placing non-essential cookies. The privacy policy discloses analytics usage but there is no mechanism for users to manage consent preferences, withdraw consent, or access a cookie preferences center. Without a functional cookie consent system, the site cannot demonstrate lawful basis for non-essential tracking.
findings.evidence: Plausible analytics loaded without consent banner: '<script async src="https://plausible.io/js/pa-zBfbav_s2OcQFGk4H2Rfd.js"></script>' in frontend/privacy.html and marketing pages. No cookie-consent library detected (e.g., OneTrust, Cookiebot, cookie-consent). Marketing HTML has no banner or modal for consent management.
findings.fix: 1. Integrate a GDPR-compliant cookie consent library (e.g., cookie-consent, Cookiebot free tier, or OneTrust basic). 2. Add a cookie banner to all pages that appears before analytics script is executed. 3. Make consent granular: Essential (always enabled), Analytics (opt-in), Marketing (opt-in). 4. Provide a way to withdraw/modify consent in footer or settings. 5. Update privacy policy with specific cookie names, purposes, and retention periods. 6. Load analytics script conditionally only after consent is granted.

findings.title: Privacy policy missing critical disclosures required by GDPR Art. 13/14
findings.description: The privacy policies (marketing/src/pages/privacy.astro and frontend/privacy.html) lack several mandatory disclosures under GDPR Articles 13 and 14: (1) No explicit legal basis for each processing activity (e.g., 'necessary for contract', 'legitimate interest', etc.). (2) No data retention periods specified for specific data types (galleries, comments, access logs). (3) No Data Protection Officer (DPO) contact information if applicable. (4) No explicit breach notification procedures or how data subjects can report a breach. (5) No information about automated decision-making or profiling. (6) No mention of recipients of personal data beyond 'AWS infrastructure' (SES, Plausible, Resend). (7) No explicit right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority.
findings.evidence: Privacy policies state: 'Gallery data remains until deleted or expired by the operator. Pending comments and operational logs are generally retained for about 30 days unless longer review is needed.' but do NOT specify: retention for access logs, sub-admin accounts, demo sessions. Email disclosure to Plausible/Resend is mentioned generically but not by name or with link to their privacy policies. No legal basis mentioned for processing client emails (contract? legitimate interest?).
findings.fix: 1. Rewrite privacy policy to explicitly state legal basis for each processing activity (e.g., 'Client account management is necessary for contract performance', 'Analytics are based on legitimate interest in service improvement'). 2. Add specific retention periods for each data type: access logs (1 year TTL), comments (30 days pending review, then 90 days archived), demo sessions (1 year TTL). 3. List all third parties with explicit links to their privacy policies (Plausible, Resend, AWS). 4. Add section on data subjects' rights (access, rectification, erasure, portability, object, automated decision-making). 5. Include DPO contact (if applicable) or responsible person for privacy inquiries. 6. Document your breach notification procedures and provide contact email for breach reports.

findings.title: GDPR data subject access (Art. 15) endpoint requires super-admin key, not accessible to data subjects
findings.description: GDPR Article 15 gives individuals the right to obtain confirmation of whether their data is being processed and to receive a copy of that data. The `/admin/gdpr/search` endpoint exists and returns all personal data for an email, but it requires the super-admin X-Admin-Key header. This means individual data subjects cannot self-serve access their data—they must contact the operator (photographer) who then must retrieve the data and manually compile it. The endpoint should be publicly accessible (or behind authentication that any individual can use) so data subjects can exercise their right independently.
findings.evidence: gdpr_search() function requires super-admin authentication. Endpoint returns all PII: 'results["tables"]["access_log"] = { "count": len(items), "fields": ["email", "ip", "action", "timestamp", "gallery_id"], "sample": items[:5] }'. Only 5 samples returned, not all data. No public/unauthenticated path to this endpoint documented in API routes.
findings.fix: 1. Create a public, unauthenticated endpoint (or one behind email-based verification) that allows individuals to request their data: POST /gdpr/access-request with email. 2. Implement email verification (send link to confirm email ownership). 3. Upon verification, return a full data export (JSON or CSV) including all records from all tables, not just samples. 4. Ensure response time meets GDPR requirement (45 days, extendable by 2 months). 5. Log the request and response delivery. Alternatively, require pre-existing client account login to access /account/export-my-data endpoint.

findings.title: GDPR data erasure (Art. 17) endpoint requires super-admin key, not data-subject-initiated
findings.description: Similar to GDPR-003, the `/admin/gdpr/erase` endpoint (GDPR Article 17 right to erasure) is only accessible to super-admins via X-Admin-Key header. Data subjects have the right to request deletion of their personal data, but this application forces them to contact the photographer/operator instead of providing a self-service mechanism. While the backend logic correctly identifies and deletes data from multiple tables, the access control prevents individuals from exercising this right independently.
findings.evidence: gdpr_erase() function checks: 'if provided.startswith("demo_"): ...' for super-admin key only. Deletion logic is comprehensive but hidden behind admin-only auth.
findings.fix: 1. Create a public/authenticated endpoint for data erasure requests: POST /gdpr/erasure-request with email and reason. 2. Implement email verification (confirmation link sent to email). 3. If reason is 'withdrawal of consent', allow self-service. If reason is 'right to be forgotten', log the request for manual review by operator. 4. After verification, proceed with gdpr_erase() logic. 5. Provide a response indicating what was deleted and timelines. 6. Log all erasure requests with audit trail.

findings.title: Access log retention period unclear and potentially indefinite
findings.description: The privacy policy states: 'Pending comments and operational logs are generally retained for about 30 days unless longer review is needed.' However, the access_log table in DynamoDB has a TTL attribute (expires_at) but the code does not set a default expiry time when inserting access log records. This means access logs could remain indefinitely unless the operator manually sets an expiry. GDPR Article 5(1)(e) requires that personal data be kept only as long as necessary. Indefinite or unclear retention violates this principle.
findings.evidence: DynamoDB table definition: 'ttl { attribute_name = "expires_at" enabled = true }' enables TTL but code that inserts access_log records does not set expires_at. See auth_view/handler.py where access logs are created but no expiry is set: access_log_table.put_item(Item={...}) without expires_at field.
findings.fix: 1. Update access log insertion code to explicitly set expires_at to current_time + 1_year (or operator-configured TTL). 2. Update privacy policy to explicitly state: 'Access logs are retained for 1 year and automatically deleted via DynamoDB TTL.' 3. For sub-admin/demo sessions, confirm default retention is also set and documented. 4. Add configuration option in environment variables to allow customization of retention periods by operator (e.g., LOG_RETENTION_DAYS=365).

findings.title: Third-party data sharing agreements and DPAs not documented
findings.description: The application uses third-party services (Plausible analytics, Resend email, AWS infrastructure) that may process personal data. GDPR Article 6(1) and Chapter III (Articles 44-49) require that data transfers be documented, and data processing agreements (DPAs) must be in place with processors. The privacy policy mentions these services generically but does not provide links to their privacy policies, confirm DPAs are in place, or address how adequacy decisions are handled. Additionally, if these services store data in the US or other non-EEA jurisdictions, Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) or other transfer mechanisms must be documented.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy: 'Plausible is the only analytics service used on these pages.' but does not link to Plausible's privacy policy or confirm a Data Processing Agreement. Code uses SES and Resend for email: 'ses = boto3.client("ses", region_name=os.environ.get("AWS_REGION", "us-west-2"))' but no documentation of DPA with AWS SES. Resend integration in code not visible in handlers reviewed.
findings.fix: 1. Review and confirm DPAs are in place with: AWS (for Lambda, DynamoDB, S3, SES), Plausible (or switch to privacy-friendly alternative like Fathom or Metrical), Resend (if used for transactional emails). 2. Document the legal basis for data transfers (e.g., SCCs, adequacy decision, Binding Corporate Rules). 3. Publish links to third-party privacy policies in your privacy policy. 4. Add section: 'Data processors and transfers: Our data is processed by AWS (Standard Contractual Clauses), Plausible (Privacy Shield or SCCs), and Resend (SCCs).' 5. Maintain a Data Processing Inventory documenting all vendors and their contracts.

findings.title: Admin API CORS configuration allows all origins (*)
findings.description: The API Gateway CORS configuration in terraform/api_gateway.tf explicitly sets: 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *' when responding to admin and auth endpoints. This allows any website to make cross-origin requests to the API from any origin. While the backend still requires authentication (X-Admin-Key or Bearer JWT), the permissive CORS headers increase attack surface: (1) Credential-based attacks can be crafted from any origin. (2) Browser-based CSRF attacks are not protected by CORS. (3) Mistake in authorization logic can be exploited from anywhere. Additionally, the admin handler also sets CORS_ORIGIN from an environment variable with default '*'.
findings.evidence: Terraform: 'allow_origins = [ "https://${local.marketing_domain}", "https://${var.marketing_www_domain}", "https://${local.gallery_domain}" ]' is correctly scoped in API Gateway. However, lambda/admin/handler.py line 454: '"Access-Control-Allow-Origin": os.environ.get("CORS_ORIGIN", "*")' sets default to wildcard. This header overrides the API Gateway CORS configuration.
findings.fix: 1. Remove the CORS_ORIGIN environment variable default '*' in lambda/admin/handler.py. Instead, whitelist specific origins: 'ALLOWED_ORIGINS = [os.environ.get("CORS_ORIGIN", "")].filter(bool)' or maintain a hardcoded list. 2. Update the respond() function to check if origin is in whitelist before setting Access-Control-Allow-Origin header. 3. Set CORS_ORIGIN environment variable to the specific gallery domain(s) only. 4. Add X-Requested-With: XMLHttpRequest check for state-changing requests (POST, DELETE). 5. Test that requests from unauthorized origins receive no CORS headers.

findings.title: Access logs and CloudWatch logs include PII (email, IP) without redaction
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.15 — Logging and Audit Trails
findings.description: Access log records include raw email and IP addresses in CloudWatch logs: 'access_log_table.put_item(Item={..."email": email..."ip": ip...})'. These logs are queryable and may be retained longer than necessary. Additionally, API Gateway logs in CloudWatch include source IP: '"sourceIp": "$context.identity.sourceIp"'. While audit logging is important, GDPR and ISO 27001 A.8.15 require that logs be minimized for PII and retention be limited. Logs containing PII should be subject to the same retention and deletion policies as other personal data.
findings.evidence: API Gateway logs include sourceIp. Access log DynamoDB table stores email and IP without aggregation or anonymization. No explicit log retention policy mentioned beyond CloudWatch default (depends on log group retention settings).
findings.fix: 1. Update access_log insertion to anonymize or hash email/IP: use hashlib.sha256(email.encode()).hexdigest() instead of raw email. 2. For rate limiting and abuse detection that require IPs, hash the IP as well. 3. Set CloudWatch log retention to match data retention policy (1 year for access logs, 30 days for debug logs). 4. Add a field to distinguish log types: 'type': 'access_attempt', 'failed_auth', 'api_error' to allow filtering sensitive logs. 5. Implement log scrubbing to redact PII from error messages before they're logged.

findings.title: Rate limiting not enforced on GDPR endpoints (/admin/gdpr/search, /admin/gdpr/erase)
findings.description: The /admin/gdpr/search and /admin/gdpr/erase endpoints require super-admin key authentication but do not have per-endpoint rate limiting beyond the global WAF (1000 req / 5 min per IP) and API Gateway defaults (100 req/sec, 200 burst). An attacker with the super-admin key (or who obtains it via compromise) could invoke these endpoints repeatedly to enumerate all data subjects in the system or to trigger bulk deletion. Additionally, the ratelimit_table in DynamoDB has server_side_encryption enabled but no point_in_time_recovery, which could allow data loss in case of attack or error.
findings.evidence: gdpr_search() and gdpr_erase() functions do not check a local rate limit cache. They perform full table scans: '_scan_all(access_log_table, ...)' which could be resource-expensive. No explicit rate limiting logic or token bucket per function.
findings.fix: 1. Add rate limiting to GDPR endpoints: check ratelimit_table before processing, increment counter per email/IP. E.g., 'max 5 access requests per email per hour', 'max 2 erasure requests per email per 24 hours'. 2. Return 429 Too Many Requests if limit exceeded. 3. Enable point_in_time_recovery on ratelimit_table: 'point_in_time_recovery { enabled = true }' in terraform/dynamodb.tf. 4. Log all GDPR requests with detailed audit trail (who, when, what data, outcome). 5. Consider requiring explicit confirmation (second factor) for erasure requests.

findings.title: CloudFront signed URLs use SHA1 for signing (deprecated cryptographic hash)
findings.description: CloudFront signed URL generation uses SHA1 (PKCS#1 v1.5 padding) to sign URLs: 'signature = private_key.sign(policy.encode(), padding.PKCS1v15(), hashes.SHA1())'. SHA1 is deprecated and no longer recommended for cryptographic purposes due to collision vulnerabilities. While CloudFront still supports SHA1 and collisions are difficult to produce in this specific context (signing structured policy documents), using a deprecated algorithm violates ISO 27001 A.8.3 cryptographic controls best practice. This is also noted in pyproject.toml as an exception: 'S303 — SHA1 required by CloudFront signed URLs'.
findings.evidence: auth_view/handler.py: 'signature = private_key.sign(policy.encode(), padding.PKCS1v15(), hashes.SHA1())'. pyproject.toml: 'S303 # SHA1 for CF signing' indicates acknowledgment of this limitation.
findings.fix: 1. Monitor CloudFront and AWS documentation for support of SHA256 signing (may require changes to CloudFront key pair type or region support). 2. If SHA256 becomes available, migrate to it: 'hashes.SHA256()' instead of 'hashes.SHA1()'. 3. For now, document this as a 'known limitation' due to CloudFront API constraints and confirm with AWS that SHA1 is secure enough for this use case. 4. Add a note to security.astro or security documentation explaining the trade-off.

findings.title: No explicit requirement for TLS 1.3 or minimum version enforcement
findings.description: While HSTS and signed URLs enforce HTTPS, there is no explicit minimum TLS version requirement in the CloudFront or API Gateway configuration. The default AWS behavior should be TLS 1.2+, but best practice (ISO 27001 A.8.24) is to explicitly require TLS 1.3 or at minimum TLS 1.2, and disable older versions. Additionally, no explicit mention of certificate pinning for mobile clients (if any).
findings.evidence: No minimum_protocol_version set in CloudFront distribution. No tls_version requirement in API Gateway.
findings.fix: 1. In CloudFront distribution resource, add: 'minimum_protocol_version = "TLSv1.2_2021-06"' (or TLSv1.3 if available). 2. In API Gateway, confirm default TLS version is 1.2+ via AWS documentation or API. 3. Consider adding Security-Headers HPKP (Public Key Pinning) header if mobile clients are used. 4. Test with nmap or testssl.sh to confirm TLS 1.0/1.1 are disabled.

findings.title: No comprehensive audit trail for administrator actions
findings.description: The admin Lambda logs some operations (create gallery, delete gallery, GDPR actions) to CloudWatch, but there is no centralized audit trail that explicitly tracks all admin actions with sufficient detail (who, what, when, where, result). For example, gallery updates, photo approval/rejection, comment moderation, and team member creation are not explicitly logged with user identity and timestamp.
findings.evidence: Some log entries: 'logger.info("Created gallery %s for client: %s", gallery_id, client_name)' but not all operations are logged. Gallery photo updates, comment moderation, sub-admin creation have no explicit logging visible in reviewed sections.
findings.fix: 1. Create a dedicated audit_log function that logs all admin actions: 'def audit_log(action, resource_id, user_id, result, details={})'. 2. Call this function for: create/update/delete gallery, approve/reject photo, moderate comment, create/modify sub-admin, generate tokens. 3. Store audit logs in a separate immutable table or CloudWatch log group with long retention (3-7 years). 4. Include fields: timestamp, action, resource_id, user_id, IP, result (success/failure), details. 5. Implement log aggregation and alerting for suspicious patterns (e.g., multiple failed auth attempts, mass deletions).

findings.title: No documented incident response or breach notification procedure
findings.description: There is no documented incident response plan, breach notification procedure, or runbook for handling security incidents. The security.astro page mentions a security contact email but does not provide procedures for how incidents are triaged, investigated, or how users are notified in case of a breach. GDPR Article 33 requires notification to supervisory authorities within 72 hours of discovering a personal data breach.
findings.evidence: Security page: 'Report security concerns to security@codyjo.com' but no procedures documented. No incident response playbook found in codebase. No references to breach notification templates or timelines.
findings.fix: 1. Create docs/INCIDENT-RESPONSE.md documenting: (a) How security issues are reported (email, responsible person). (b) Initial triage and severity assessment. (c) Investigation steps and forensic procedures. (d) Notification timelines (internal: immediate, DPA: 72 hours, users: without undue delay). (e) Communication templates for affected users. (f) Remediation and recovery steps. 2. Create docs/BREACH-NOTIFICATION.md with breach notification template that includes: nature of breach, categories of personal data, likely consequences, measures taken to mitigate. 3. Set up CloudWatch alarms for suspicious activities (high rate of deletions, auth failures, WAF blocks) and route them to the security team via SNS.

findings.title: No Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) documented
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 35 — Data Protection Impact Assessment
findings.description: GDPR Article 35 requires that a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) be conducted for processing that is 'likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons'. Analogify processes personal photos, email addresses, IP addresses, and activity data, and may involve automated processing (e.g., photo analysis by ChromaHaus). This is exactly the type of processing that typically requires a DPIA. There is no evidence of a DPIA being conducted or documented.
findings.evidence: No docs/DPIA.md or similar file found in codebase. Recital 75 of GDPR guidance suggests DPIAs for: large-scale processing of special categories, systematic monitoring, automated decision-making. ChromaHaus photo analysis could be considered automated processing (though not decision-making).
findings.fix: 1. Conduct a DPIA following the EDPB guidelines (EDPB Template or ISO/IEC 27550). 2. Document processing activities: (a) Identification of data controller/processor. (b) Purposes of processing. (c) Legitimate interest assessment (if applicable). (d) Categories of data subjects and personal data. (e) Risk assessment: identify risks to individuals' rights and freedoms. (f) Mitigation measures (technical and organizational). (g) Necessity and proportionality assessment. 3. Store the DPIA in docs/DPIA.md and reference it in the privacy policy. 4. If risks are identified as high, consult with supervisory authority per Article 36.

findings.title: No Data Protection Officer (DPO) role or contact information
findings.description: GDPR Article 37 requires certain organizations to designate a Data Protection Officer (DPO). While Analogify is likely a small organization and may be exempt, the privacy policy does not clarify whether a DPO is designated or who the responsible person is for privacy inquiries. This creates ambiguity for individuals trying to exercise their rights or contact the organization about privacy concerns.
findings.evidence: Privacy contact: 'Questions or privacy requests can be sent to cody@codyjo.com' but no mention of whether Cody is a DPO or the responsible person.
findings.fix: 1. Determine if Analogify meets DPO requirement criteria under GDPR Article 37(1) (public authority, core activity is systematic monitoring, large-scale processing of special categories). 2. If exempt, add to privacy policy: 'A Data Protection Officer is not required under GDPR Article 37. Privacy inquiries can be directed to: cody@codyjo.com'. 3. If DPO is required or voluntarily designated, add to privacy policy: 'Our Data Protection Officer is [Name] ([email])' and ensure DPO contact is publicly available.

findings.title: Age verification not applicable to this project
findings.regulation: US State Laws (Louisiana, Utah, Texas, Virginia, Arkansas, Montana, California), UK Online Safety Act
findings.description: Analogify Studio is a B2B photographer gallery delivery platform. It does not serve content directly to end consumers, nor does it host adult content, social media functionality, or content restricted by age. The platform is designed for photographers to deliver client galleries, which are access-controlled and requires gallery owner authentication. Individual photographers are responsible for compliance with age verification laws if they serve minors. The platform itself does not need to implement age verification gates.
findings.evidence: Platform architecture: photographers authenticate with admin panel, create galleries, clients authenticate via email-based entry. No public content, no age-restricted content, no social features, no user-to-user interaction beyond moderated comments. Analogify Zen (consumer product) is marketing-only and has no backend.
findings.fix: No action required. However, if in future the platform adds: (1) a public gallery directory with uncontrolled access, (2) social media features, (3) age-restricted content delivery, or (4) consumer-facing apps (Analogify Zen backend), age verification may become applicable.

findings.title: Secrets and API keys stored in plaintext in configuration files
findings.description: The backoffice.yaml configuration file (intentionally gitignored) stores sensitive values in plaintext: AWS access/secret keys, CloudFront distribution IDs, API keys, and database credentials. If this file is compromised, an attacker gains full access to AWS infrastructure, dashboards, and potentially target repositories. No encryption at rest is applied to configuration data.
findings.evidence: config/backoffice.example.yaml shows: api_key: "", aws region/bucket/distribution credentials all stored as plaintext YAML. The config loader (backoffice/config.py) reads these values directly into dataclasses with no decryption step.
findings.fix: 1. Integrate AWS Secrets Manager or HashiCorp Vault for secrets storage. 2. Modify backoffice/config.py to fetch sensitive values from the secrets manager at startup. 3. Use environment variable references in backoffice.yaml for sensitive fields (e.g., api_key: "${API_KEY}"). 4. Document the secrets management approach in setup instructions. 5. Add pre-commit hook to prevent accidental commits of plaintext secrets.

findings.title: No enforcement of HTTPS/TLS for API server
findings.description: The api_server.py and server.py use Python's http.server (HTTP only). There is no built-in TLS/SSL support. If deployed directly (not behind a reverse proxy with TLS termination), all API traffic is sent in plaintext, exposing API keys, findings data, and scan requests.
findings.evidence: api_server.py line 1 uses http.server.BaseHTTPRequestHandler without ssl module wrapper. No SSL context creation or certificate loading. README deployment section does not explicitly require reverse proxy with HTTPS.
findings.fix: 1. Document that the API server MUST be deployed behind a reverse proxy (nginx, Apache, AWS ALB) with TLS termination. 2. Alternatively, add ssl.wrap_socket() context to api_server.py if direct HTTPS is needed. 3. Add a startup check that logs a WARNING if api_key is configured but no reverse proxy is detected. 4. Update deployment documentation to include HTTPS-required notation. 5. Add security headers (HSTS, CSP) in the reverse proxy configuration.

findings.title: No rate limiting on API endpoints
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.2 (User Authentication Management) - Rate Limiting
findings.description: The API server (api_server.py) accepts requests without rate limiting. An attacker can brute-force API keys, enumerate scan endpoints, or trigger denial-of-service by spawning unlimited concurrent agent jobs (each consuming CPU/memory).
findings.evidence: do_POST() method accepts any request and calls run_agent() without checking request rate. No per-IP or per-key rate limiting. No request queuing or backpressure mechanism.
findings.fix: 1. Implement per-IP rate limiting using a library like python-ratelimit or Flask-Limiter (even though this is a raw http.server). 2. Add a simple in-memory token bucket or sliding window counter keyed by request origin. 3. Return HTTP 429 (Too Many Requests) when rate limit is exceeded. 4. Recommend deploying behind a reverse proxy with rate limiting (nginx limit_req, AWS WAF). 5. Document rate limit thresholds in the API documentation.

findings.title: Insufficient audit logging of security-relevant events
findings.description: While backoffice/log_config.py provides structured logging, security-relevant events are not comprehensively logged: failed API authentication attempts are not explicitly logged, API key usage is not tracked, unauthorized endpoint access is not recorded, and data access patterns are not logged. Logs may also contain exception stacktraces with sensitive information (file paths, internal structure).
findings.evidence: api_server.py _check_auth() returns True/False but does not log failed auth attempts. No audit trail of which API keys accessed which endpoints. No request/response logging in APIHandler.
findings.fix: 1. Add explicit logging for authentication failures: log failed API key attempts with source IP and timestamp. 2. Log all API requests with method, path, source IP, API key ID (not value), status code, and response size. 3. Add separate audit log file (audit.log) with immutable append-only semantics. 4. Sanitize exception logs to avoid exposing file paths, config values, or internal structure. 5. Implement log retention and rotation policies.

findings.title: No API key rotation mechanism
findings.description: API keys are stored in backoffice.yaml and read at startup. There is no rotation schedule, no expiration policy, and no mechanism to revoke compromised keys. If a key is leaked, the only remedy is manual file editing and service restart.
findings.evidence: api_key stored as static string in config. APIHandler._check_auth() compares against a single key. No key versioning, rotation, or expiration.
findings.fix: 1. Implement API key rotation: support multiple active keys with expiration dates. 2. Move keys to a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). 3. Add --rotate-key command to CLI to generate new keys. 4. Log all key rotations and revocations. 5. Document a key rotation policy (e.g., rotate every 90 days).

findings.title: Missing Content-Security-Policy and security headers on dashboard
findings.description: Dashboard HTML files (e.g., dashboard/index.html) include a basic CSP header but it allows unsafe-inline for scripts and styles. This permits XSS attacks if any user-controlled data (e.g., findings from audited repos) is displayed without proper escaping. Additionally, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy, and cache-control headers are missing.
findings.evidence: index.html: CSP includes 'unsafe-inline' for script-src and style-src. This allows inline event handlers and style attributes, enabling XSS. Connect-src is 'self' only, which is good.
findings.fix: 1. Upgrade CSP to remove 'unsafe-inline' for scripts: use nonce-based or hash-based script execution. 2. Move inline styles to external stylesheets. 3. Add X-Frame-Options: DENY to prevent clickjacking. 4. Add X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff to prevent MIME sniffing. 5. Add Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin. 6. Add Permissions-Policy to disable sensitive browser features (geolocation, camera, microphone). 7. For sensitive pages, add Cache-Control: no-store, private.

findings.title: Insufficient input validation on TARGET parameter in agent scripts
findings.description: The api_server.py and shell scripts accept a 'target' parameter from the request body. While Python's subprocess.run() with a list avoids shell injection, the target path is not validated against a whitelist. An attacker could potentially specify an arbitrary filesystem path (e.g., /etc/passwd) as a target, causing unexpected behavior.
findings.evidence: api_server.py _handle_scan() reads target from POST body and passes to run_agent(). Target is resolved via resolve_target() but the resolution is permissive: any existing directory path is accepted. No whitelist check against configured targets.
findings.fix: 1. Validate that the supplied target name matches one of the configured targets in backoffice.yaml. 2. Reject any target path that is not explicitly configured. 3. Add an explicit whitelist check before calling run_agent(). 4. Log rejected scan attempts with the supplied target and source IP.

findings.title: CORS configuration could be overly permissive if misconfigured
findings.description: api_server.py supports wildcard CORS origin ('*' in allowed_origins). While the default config (backoffice.example.yaml) specifies legitimate origins, a misconfigured deployment could expose the API to requests from any origin, allowing cross-site request forgery attacks.
findings.evidence: api_server.py _cors_headers() checks: if '*' in allowed or origin in allowed. This allows the config to specify '*' as an allowed origin, which is overly permissive for a sensitive API.
findings.fix: 1. Update backoffice/api_server.py to reject '*' as an allowed origin (comment out or remove the wildcard check). 2. Document that allowed_origins must be an explicit list of trusted domains. 3. Add a validation check during config loading to reject wildcard origins and log a warning. 4. Recommend deploying with specific, domain-restricted CORS origins only.

findings.title: Registration consent is required in the UI but not recorded server-side
findings.description: The registration form requires a privacy-policy checkbox, but the submitted payload contains only name, email, password, and preferredVersion, and the backend profile record stores no consent timestamp, policy version, or consent scope. That leaves the service unable to evidence that consent was freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous.
findings.evidence: The checkbox at lines 178-183 is mandatory, but the POST body at lines 71-76 omits any consent fields. The backend profile written in lambda/api/index.mjs lines 416-431 stores email, name, passwordHash, salt, preferredVersion, emailVerified, and createdAt only.
findings.fix: Add explicit consent fields to the register payload, store consent scope, timestamp, and policy/version identifiers in DynamoDB, and expose withdrawal/preference management for any optional processing tied to consent.

findings.title: Third-party analytics loads before any consent or preference flow
findings.description: The root layout injects Plausible analytics for every visitor as soon as the app loads. The repository contains no consent banner, analytics preference center, or conditional loading path tied to user choice. Even for cookieless analytics, this creates a GDPR transparency and lawful-basis gap unless the operator has documented and disclosed a defensible alternative basis outside the codebase.
findings.evidence: layout.tsx lines 66-67 load plausible.io globally. A repo-wide search found no cookie banner or consent-management implementation under src/app, src/components, or src/lib.
findings.fix: Implement a consent/preferences layer for analytics and gate Plausible script injection on that preference, or remove third-party analytics for regulated users and update privacy disclosures accordingly.

findings.title: The backend supports account deletion, but the app exposes no user-facing erasure flow
findings.description: The API provides an authenticated DELETE /api/account route that removes account data, but the Settings page exposes sign-out, backup, import/export, and password-change controls only. There is no in-product delete-account or erase-my-data path, which makes the right to erasure materially harder to exercise.
findings.evidence: The account UI at lines 223-245 shows profile details and a sign-out button only. The deletion endpoint exists in lambda/api/index.mjs at lines 363 and 847-905, but there is no client-side call to /api/account in the repository.
findings.fix: Add a delete-account flow in Settings that requires password re-entry, calls DELETE /api/account, clears local data, and confirms what data will be removed.

findings.title: No retention limit is applied to persisted account data
findings.description: The DynamoDB table supports TTL only through the expiresAt attribute, but user profile, study, journal, bookmark, and daily records are written without any retention field. The repo contains no cleanup job or retention-policy implementation for personal data stored in the account backend.
findings.evidence: User content is stored in lambda/api/index.mjs lines 1001-1015, 1092-1130, 1183-1195, and 1260-1271 without expiresAt. terraform/dynamodb.tf lines 38-41 enable TTL only for records that explicitly include expiresAt.
findings.fix: Define retention periods by data type, store expiry metadata where appropriate, and add scheduled purge logic or lifecycle jobs. This requires business and legal input before code changes.

findings.title: Bearer tokens and user profile data are persisted in localStorage
findings.description: Authentication tokens, user profile data, and cached encryption metadata are stored in browser localStorage. Because localStorage is readable by any injected JavaScript, a future XSS defect or compromised third-party script would expose active sessions and user identifiers. This is weaker than an HttpOnly session-cookie model for a production consumer app processing account and journal data.
findings.evidence: auth-context.tsx stores bible_token, bible_user, and selah_enc_cache in localStorage at lines 19-21, 25-31, 49-56, and 62-71. StudyChat also reads bible_token from localStorage at src/components/StudyChat.tsx lines 136-143.
findings.fix: Move session handling to Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite cookies or another server-managed session mechanism, and keep only strictly necessary non-sensitive state in web storage.

findings.title: Account backup uploads journal content in plaintext when the encryption key is unavailable
findings.description: The account-backup path encrypts journals only when a master key is present. If the user is signed in but the in-memory key is unavailable, the backup routine sends the raw journal entry to the API, and the backend stores plaintext content. That defeats the stated zero-knowledge journal design for a common recovery state.
findings.evidence: account-sync.ts lines 107-115 send the unmodified journal entry when masterKey is falsy. Settings calls backupLocalDataToAccount(undefined, masterKey) at src/app/settings/page.tsx lines 139-152, so a signed-in user can trigger backup even when masterKey is null.
findings.fix: Block journal backup until the journal key is unlocked, or split backup so journals are skipped with a clear warning unless encryption is active. On the backend, reject plaintext journal writes for accounts that have encryption enabled.

findings.title: Content Security Policy allows unsafe inline and eval execution
findings.description: The CloudFront security headers policy sets a CSP, but it permits both 'unsafe-inline' and 'unsafe-eval' in script-src. Those exceptions materially weaken XSS defenses, especially because the frontend also stores bearer tokens in browser storage and loads third-party scripts.
findings.evidence: The CSP at terraform/cloudfront.tf lines 218-220 is: script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' 'unsafe-eval' https://plausible.io.
findings.fix: Remove unsafe-inline and unsafe-eval, migrate any inline bootstrapping to nonce- or hash-based scripts, and confirm the build/runtime path works without eval-dependent tooling in production.

findings.title: Privacy policy missing data retention period, DPO contact, and specific lawful basis
findings.description: The privacy policy at /privacy.astro discloses data collection and third parties but omits: (1) specific retention period for contact form data, (2) Data Protection Officer contact information, (3) explicit lawful basis per processing activity (Article 6), (4) breach notification procedures. GDPR Articles 13-14 require complete transparency at the point of data collection.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy states general purpose ('to respond to that request') but not: retention duration for contact submissions, lawful basis selection (consent vs. legitimate interest), DPO email if applicable, or breach notification procedure. No mention of retention policy beyond generic 'request-based deletion'.
findings.fix: Expand privacy policy to include: (1) 'Contact form data is retained for [30-90] days or until inquiry is resolved, whichever is longer, then deleted.' (2) 'We process contact data on the basis of [consent given via checkbox / legitimate interest in responding to inquiries].' (3) DPO contact if applicable, or clarify no separate DPO is appointed. (4) 'Suspected breaches are reported to [supervisory authority] within 72 hours. Contact privacy@codyjo.com for security incidents.'

findings.title: No documented mechanism for users to exercise data subject rights (access, deletion, portability)
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 15-22 — Data Subject Rights Mechanism
findings.description: Privacy policy states users can request access, correction, or deletion, but provides no email address, contact form, or API endpoint for submitting such requests. GDPR Articles 15-22 require controllers to establish a practical mechanism for data subjects to exercise their rights.
findings.evidence: Lines 117-121 mention rights exist but offer no submission mechanism. No privacy@codyjo.com email address, dedicated rights-request endpoint, or documented procedure for processing access/deletion requests is provided.
findings.fix: Add to privacy policy: 'To exercise your data subject rights, email privacy@codyjo.com with your request. Requests for access will return contact form submissions, timestamps, and consent records within 30 days. Requests for deletion will remove all personal data unless we have a legal obligation to retain it.'

findings.title: No documented Data Processing Agreement (DPA) with Plausible Analytics processor
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 28 — Data Processing Agreement for Plausible
findings.description: Privacy policy discloses Plausible but does not confirm a signed Data Processing Agreement. GDPR Article 28 requires a DPA when a processor handles personal data on behalf of the controller. Plausible typically offers a standard DPA, but lack of documented agreement creates compliance risk.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy lists Plausible with a data policy link but does not state 'We have executed a Data Processing Agreement with Plausible' or reference a DPA addendum. No evidence of DPA in the repository.
findings.fix: Verify Plausible DPA is in place (typically included in their standard terms). Update privacy policy to state: 'Plausible is our data processor under a Data Processing Agreement that complies with GDPR Article 28. Plausible does not process personal data for any purpose other than providing analytics services to us.'

findings.title: No documented legal mechanism for US data transfers (Schrems II compliance)
findings.description: Contact form data is sent to AWS Lambda in us-west-2. Privacy policy mentions US hosting but does not reference Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs), adequacy decisions, or equivalent safeguards required by GDPR Articles 44-49. Post-Schrems II, inadequate transfer mechanisms expose the controller to enforcement action.
findings.evidence: API endpoint at line 317: https://ldnybm21m6.execute-api.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/contact points to US infrastructure. Privacy policy (line 43) states 'Infrastructure is primarily hosted in the United States' but does not reference SCCs, adequacy decisions, or supplementary measures.
findings.fix: Confirm AWS Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) are in place via AWS Data Processing Addendum. Update privacy policy: 'Personal data is transferred to the United States under Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) that ensure adequate safeguards. You may request details of the transfer mechanism by contacting privacy@codyjo.com.'

findings.title: No clear mechanism for users to withdraw contact form consent
findings.description: Contact form requires consent checkbox but privacy policy does not explain how to withdraw consent or what happens to data upon withdrawal. GDPR Article 7(3) requires withdrawal to be as easy as giving consent.
findings.evidence: Consent checkbox at line 280 but no explanation in privacy policy on how to withdraw consent, revoke permission, or request data deletion after submission. No contact method provided for withdrawal requests.
findings.fix: Update privacy policy to include: 'You can withdraw your consent at any time by contacting privacy@codyjo.com. Upon withdrawal, we will delete your contact form data unless we have a legal obligation to retain it.'

findings.title: CSP header contains 'unsafe-inline' for scripts, weakening XSS protection
findings.description: Content-Security-Policy header in Base.astro line 66 includes script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' https://plausible.io. The 'unsafe-inline' directive defeats XSS protection by allowing arbitrary inline script execution without nonce or hash validation. This significantly weakens browser-enforced security for a site collecting contact data.
findings.evidence: CSP meta tag: "script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' https://plausible.io;" The 'unsafe-inline' allows inline <script> tags to execute without restrictions, nullifying CSP's XSS mitigation value.
findings.fix: Remove 'unsafe-inline' from script-src and use nonces: (1) Add a nonce to Plausible script tag (line 123). (2) Astro should support automatic nonce generation. (3) Update CSP to: script-src 'self' 'nonce-[generated]' https://plausible.io;. (4) Move inline Plausible init code to external file or apply nonce.

findings.title: Missing HTTP security headers: X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy
findings.description: Site does not explicitly configure several important HTTP security headers to prevent clickjacking, MIME-sniffing, referrer leakage, and browser feature abuse. While CloudFront may apply defaults, relying on implicit CDN behavior rather than explicit configuration is a compliance gap.
findings.evidence: CSP is set via meta tag (line 66) but no X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy, or Permissions-Policy headers are configured. These should be set at the HTTP response level (CloudFront policy or middleware).
findings.fix: Configure CloudFront response headers policy or Astro middleware to set: (1) X-Frame-Options: DENY (prevent clickjacking). (2) X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff (prevent MIME-sniffing). (3) Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin (limit referrer leakage). (4) Permissions-Policy: camera=(), microphone=(), geolocation=() (restrict browser features).

findings.title: Contact form client-side validation only; server-side validation not visible
findings.description: Contact form implements client-side validation (email regex, required fields, length limits) but the AWS Lambda backend code is not in the repository. Server-side validation cannot be verified, creating risk of malformed or injection-attack payloads being stored.
findings.evidence: Email validation at line 338-342 uses client-side regex. Lambda endpoint at ldnybm21m6.execute-api.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/contact is external and not auditable from the repository.
findings.fix: Verify that the AWS Lambda function implements server-side validation: (1) Email format (robust regex or validation library). (2) Maximum length checks. (3) Required field validation. (4) Character restrictions (reject control characters, unusual Unicode). (5) XSS prevention (escape HTML entities before storage). Return 400/422 if validation fails.

findings.title: Contact API endpoint lacks documented authentication or authorization
findings.description: Contact form submits to AWS API Gateway endpoint with no visible authentication (no API key, bearer token, or OAuth). While honeypot and rate-limiting provide basic protection, a public endpoint without authentication can be abused for spam, DoS, or malicious data injection.
findings.evidence: Fetch request at line 362 sends JSON with Content-Type header only; no Authorization header or API key visible. No evidence of API Gateway IAM authorization or Lambda authorization logic in the repository.
findings.fix: Implement API authentication: (1) Add API key validation in Lambda (check x-api-key header against environment variable). (2) Or use AWS API Gateway Lambda authorizer with IAM role. (3) Verify CORS is restricted to https://www.codyjo.com origin. (4) Confirm 429 rate-limiting is in place (already implemented per code).

findings.title: No documented retention period for server and CDN logs
findings.description: Privacy policy mentions 'server-side and CDN request logs' but does not specify retention duration. Log retention policy is essential for audit trails and incident investigation per ISO 27001 A.5.11.
findings.evidence: Lines 67-72 describe logs but state only that they are 'used for site reliability, abuse prevention, and troubleshooting' without specifying retention period (e.g., '90 days').
findings.fix: Update privacy policy: 'Server and CDN logs are retained for 90 days in accordance with AWS CloudFront default retention and used for security investigation and troubleshooting. Logs are not shared with third parties unless required by law.'

findings.title: CSP allows overly broad AWS API Gateway endpoint pattern
findings.description: CSP includes connect-src https://*.execute-api.us-west-2.amazonaws.com, allowing connections to any API Gateway endpoint in the region. This is broader than necessary and could allow requests to unintended endpoints if an attacker compromises the site's JavaScript context.
findings.evidence: CSP at line 66: connect-src 'self' https://*.execute-api.us-west-2.amazonaws.com https://plausible.io; The wildcard allows any endpoint in the region.
findings.fix: Restrict CSP to the specific API endpoint: connect-src 'self' https://ldnybm21m6.execute-api.us-west-2.amazonaws.com https://plausible.io; If the endpoint domain is managed externally, document why the wildcard is necessary.

findings.title: No documented Record of Processing Activities (RoPA)
findings.description: GDPR Article 30 requires controllers to maintain a Record of Processing Activities. While small sites may have simple records, there is no evidence of a formal RoPA document in the repository or accompanying documentation.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy describes processing in narrative form but no structured RoPA document (CSV, table, or JSON) listing data categories, purposes, retention, recipients, and legal basis is visible.
findings.fix: Create a simple Record of Processing Activities (internal document, not public) listing: (1) Contact form: name/email/message, purpose: respond to inquiry, retention: 30-90 days, basis: consent, no sharing. (2) Plausible analytics: aggregated usage, purpose: understand traffic, retention: per Plausible policy, basis: consent or legitimate interest, processor: Plausible.

findings.title: Missing privacy policy and data processing disclosure
findings.description: The pe-bootstrap web UI collects personal data (requestor name, scenario description, console password, deployment preferences) but provides no privacy policy, data processing notice, or transparency about how data is handled. Users are not informed of their data subject rights, retention periods, or legal basis for processing. A privacy policy is required by GDPR Art. 13 (pre-collection disclosure) and Art. 14 (post-collection disclosure if data wasn't directly collected from the data subject).
findings.evidence: No privacy policy link in frontend UI. No disclosure in the deployment form about data collection, storage, or retention. Variables collected: requestor (Art. 4(1) personal data), scenario (may contain personal data), console_password (sensitive data).
findings.fix: 1. Create a privacy policy page (e.g., frontend/web/privacy.html) that discloses: (a) controller identity and contact details, (b) purposes of processing (infrastructure deployment, audit logging), (c) legal basis (Art. 6 — likely legitimate interest or contract), (d) categories of personal data collected, (e) retention periods, (f) data subject rights and how to exercise them, (g) recipients if data is shared. 2. Link the privacy policy from the UI (footer or settings). 3. Add a notice on the deployment form explaining data handling. 4. Update API documentation with data handling expectations.

findings.title: No documented lawful basis for personal data processing
findings.description: The system collects and processes personal data (requestor name, email from Google OAuth, scenario descriptions, console password) without establishing or documenting a lawful basis under GDPR Art. 6. The system does not explicitly ask for consent, disclose legitimate interests, or establish a contractual relationship. Without a clear lawful basis, the processing is unlawful.
findings.evidence: Auth token handling at line 630 accepts tokens without consent flow. Personal data stored in metadata (lines 690–715 in main.py) with no lawful basis notation. Google OAuth integration (lines 567–579) but no disclosure of data use or consent.
findings.fix: 1. Determine the lawful basis for each processing activity: Likely candidates are (a) Art. 6(1)(b) if pe-bootstrap is part of an employment or service contract, (b) Art. 6(1)(f) legitimate interests (infrastructure management, security, operational need). 2. Document the basis clearly in the privacy policy. 3. If using legitimate interests, conduct and document a Legitimate Interest Assessment (LIA) showing that interests are not overridden by individual rights. 4. Update the terms of service or user agreement to reference the lawful basis. 5. For Google OAuth, clarify in the privacy policy that you are relying on consent (Art. 7) or another basis, and ensure users can withdraw consent.

findings.title: No mechanism for data subject access requests
findings.description: Users/requestors have no way to request and receive a copy of their personal data held by the system. GDPR Art. 15 grants data subjects the right to obtain a copy of their data within 30 days. The system stores requestor names, scenario descriptions, deployment metadata, and audit trails in GCS but provides no way for users to export or retrieve this data.
findings.evidence: No API endpoint for data export (GET /api/user/data or similar). Metadata stored in GCS (line 403–414) is readable only by administrators with GCS access, not by end users.
findings.fix: 1. Implement a /api/user/data endpoint that returns all personal data associated with the authenticated user (deployment metadata, audit log entries, etc.). 2. Return data in a structured, machine-readable format (JSON or CSV). 3. Add a button in the web UI (e.g., under Settings or Account) that allows users to download their data. 4. Document the 30-day SLA for responding to requests.

findings.title: No mechanism for data deletion or erasure
findings.description: While users can destroy their deployments, the system does not provide a way to delete the associated personal data (requestor metadata, audit trails, deployment records) from the system. GDPR Art. 17 grants the right to erasure (except where data must be retained for legal/contractual reasons). Metadata remains in GCS indefinitely after deployment destruction.
findings.evidence: destroy_deployment endpoint (line 792) destroys GCP infrastructure but does not delete metadata or audit logs from GCS. Metadata remains in GCS at deployments/{deployment_id}/metadata.json indefinitely.
findings.fix: 1. Implement a delete endpoint /api/deployments/{deployment_id}/delete that (a) verifies the authenticated user owns the deployment, (b) deletes metadata, outputs, and audit logs from GCS, (c) returns a confirmation. 2. Create a retention policy: offer a 30-day grace period before hard deletion, then purge all data. 3. Add a 'delete my data' option in the web UI. 4. Document the retention period in the privacy policy.

findings.title: No documented data retention policy
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 5(1)(e), Art. 25 (Storage Limitation & Data Protection by Design)
findings.description: The system collects and stores personal data (requestor metadata, audit trails, deployment outputs) with no documented retention policy or automatic cleanup mechanism. GDPR Art. 5(1)(e) requires that personal data be kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than necessary. The system appears to retain deployment metadata indefinitely.
findings.evidence: Metadata is written to GCS (line 403) and read indefinitely. A TTL field exists in metadata (line 709) but it appears to be informational only (for deployment lifecycle, not data retention). No cleanup job or lifecycle policy is enforced on GCS blobs.
findings.fix: 1. Define a data retention policy: (a) Deployment metadata: retain for 90 days post-deployment-destruction, then delete. (b) Audit trails: retain for 1 year for compliance/security purposes, then delete. 2. Implement automated deletion via GCP Cloud Scheduler + Cloud Functions or GCS lifecycle rules. 3. Document the retention policy in the privacy policy and CLAUDE.md. 4. Add a /api/user/data-retention endpoint that discloses the retention schedule to users.

findings.title: Missing security headers and TLS enforcement
findings.description: The web UI does not implement critical HTTP security headers (Content-Security-Policy, Strict-Transport-Security, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy) that are required by GDPR Art. 32 (security measures commensurate with the level of risk). Absence of these headers increases vulnerability to XSS, clickjacking, and data interception.
findings.evidence: Flask app (line 24) does not set security headers. CORS(app) at line 25 is configured without explicit origin restrictions. No Content-Security-Policy or HSTS header configuration observed.
findings.fix: 1. Add security headers to Flask responses via middleware or after_request hook: (a) Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload (b) Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' https://accounts.google.com; img-src 'self' data: (c) X-Frame-Options: DENY (d) X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff (e) Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin (f) Cache-Control: private, no-store for sensitive endpoints. 2. Restrict CORS to specific origins (not wildcard). 3. Enforce HTTPS redirects.

findings.title: Overly permissive CORS configuration
findings.description: CORS is enabled with no explicit origin restrictions (CORS(app) enables all origins). This allows any website to make requests to the API on behalf of authenticated users, increasing the risk of cross-site request forgery (CSRF) and unauthorized access if an attacker can trick a user into visiting a malicious site while logged in.
findings.evidence: CORS(app) at line 25 with no origin restrictions. frontend/infra/main.tf comment acknowledges 'Cloud Run is publicly accessible (CORS requires requests to reach Flask)' but does not restrict to legitimate origins.
findings.fix: 1. Replace CORS(app) with explicit origin whitelisting: from flask_cors import CORS; CORS(app, resources={'/api/*': {'origins': ['https://pe-bootstrap.example.com']}}) 2. Identify the frontend domain(s) that will call the API and whitelist only those. 3. Set credentials=True and allow_headers=['Authorization', 'Content-Type'].

findings.title: No mechanism to correct inaccurate personal data
findings.description: Users cannot correct inaccurate requestor names or scenario descriptions after deployment creation. GDPR Art. 16 grants the right to rectification of inaccurate data. The system should allow users to update these fields.
findings.evidence: No PATCH /api/deployments/{id} endpoint to update metadata. Metadata is write-once after creation (line 717).
findings.fix: 1. Add a PATCH /api/deployments/{deployment_id} endpoint that allows users to update: scenario, requestor name. 2. Verify user ownership of the deployment before allowing updates. 3. Log all changes in the audit trail. 4. Add an edit button in the web UI (e.g., in the deployment details page).

findings.title: Audit logs stored without integrity protection
findings.description: Audit logs are appended to JSON files in GCS (append_audit_event, line 436) but there is no cryptographic proof of integrity or tamper-detection. An attacker with GCS write access could modify logs to hide their actions. ISO 27001 A.8.15 requires audit logs to be protected against unauthorized access and modification.
findings.evidence: Audit events are appended to metadata.json in GCS (lines 441–451). The file is mutable and can be overwritten by anyone with GCS write permission. No checksums, signatures, or immutable storage (e.g., Cloud Logging) used.
findings.fix: 1. Migrate audit logging to Google Cloud Logging (Stackdriver) instead of JSON files in GCS. Cloud Logging provides immutable, tamper-proof audit trails. 2. Configure log retention (e.g., 90 days for operational logs, 1 year for security-relevant logs) via log sinks. 3. Set up log analysis/alerting for suspicious events (multiple failed auth attempts, unauthorized API calls). 4. Ensure logs are accessible to security and compliance teams but not to end users.

findings.title: Session tokens stored in browser sessionStorage (sensitive)
findings.description: Authentication tokens (passphrase or Google ID token) are stored in browser sessionStorage (app.js line 82, 34), which is readable by any JavaScript on the page. While sessionStorage is cleared on browser close (better than localStorage), it is still vulnerable to XSS attacks. A malicious script can read sessionStorage and steal the token.
findings.evidence: sessionStorage.setItem('pe_auth_token', token) at line 82 and sessionStorage.getItem at line 9. No HttpOnly flag or secure storage mechanism.
findings.fix: 1. Store authentication tokens in memory only (not in sessionStorage), or use HttpOnly cookies if possible. 2. Implement rate limiting and account lockout on failed login attempts to mitigate brute-force attacks. 3. Set CSP header to restrict script sources (reduces XSS risk). 4. Consider adding a token expiration mechanism (e.g., tokens expire after 1 hour, requiring re-authentication). 5. Investigate using Web Crypto API for in-memory token management.

findings.title: No rate limiting on authentication endpoints
findings.description: The /api/auth/verify endpoint accepts unlimited auth attempts without rate limiting, enabling brute-force attacks against the passphrase. An attacker could try thousands of passphrases per second until one succeeds.
findings.evidence: auth_verify endpoint (line 625) has no rate-limiting decorator or logic. No mention of rate limiting in the Flask app configuration.
findings.fix: 1. Add rate limiting to /api/auth/verify: implement exponential backoff (e.g., 5 attempts per minute, then 1 attempt per 5 minutes after 5 failed attempts). 2. Use Flask-Limiter library: from flask_limiter import Limiter; limiter.limit('5/minute')(auth_verify). 3. Log repeated failures and alert on suspicious patterns. 4. Consider implementing account lockout: temporary lock after N failed attempts.

findings.title: Missing data export mechanism for right to data portability
findings.description: GDPR Article 20 requires that data subjects have the right to receive their personal data in a structured, commonly used, machine-readable format and to transmit it to another controller. The system has account deletion but no data export endpoint that allows users to download their data (profile info, settings, saved content, notes, journals, bookmarks, plans, preferences, etc.) in a portable format like JSON or CSV.
findings.evidence: The API routes show /api/account DELETE endpoint at line 540, but no GET /api/export or similar endpoint. The handleDeleteAccount function (line 1051) deletes all user data but does not provide export capability. User data includes: profile (name, email), studies, journals, bookmarks, daily verses, plans, streak data, preferences, event intake data.
findings.fix: Implement GET /api/export endpoint that: (1) authenticates the requesting user, (2) queries all user data from DynamoDB using the user ID, (3) assembles data into a JSON or CSV format with all personal data fields, (4) returns the export as a downloadable file. Include: user profile, all studies, all journals, all bookmarks, preferences, plan progress, streaks, and event-related data. Add a timestamp to the export showing when it was generated. Implement rate limiting to prevent abuse.

findings.title: Content Security Policy allows unsafe-inline and unsafe-eval for scripts
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.24 (Cryptography & Key Management) and OWASP Top 10 (A03:2021 Injection)
findings.description: The CSP header in CloudFront allows 'unsafe-inline' and 'unsafe-eval' for script-src, which defeats the purpose of CSP and significantly increases the attack surface for Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) injection attacks. While unsafe-inline is sometimes necessary for SPA applications, the addition of unsafe-eval is particularly dangerous and should be avoided.
findings.evidence: Line 278: content_security_policy = "default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' 'unsafe-eval' https://plausible.io; ..." The script-src directive explicitly includes both 'unsafe-inline' and 'unsafe-eval', which allow arbitrary JavaScript execution and bypass CSP protections.
findings.fix: (1) Remove 'unsafe-eval' immediately — there is almost never a legitimate reason for unsafe-eval. (2) For unsafe-inline: attempt to remove by using Content Security Policy nonces or hashes for inline scripts. If that's not feasible due to Next.js architecture, document why it's necessary and document compensating controls (e.g., strong input validation, output encoding). (3) Test that existing scripts still work with a stricter CSP (using test policy first). (4) Consider using a CSP violation reporting mechanism to catch injection attempts.

findings.title: No granular consent mechanism for non-essential cookies and analytics
findings.description: The privacy policy mentions data collection and processing, but there is no cookie consent banner or granular consent mechanism visible on the site. The login page checks for GPC (Global Privacy Control) signal, but this is browser-level rather than site-specific consent. Third-party analytics (plausible.io is mentioned in CSP) may be loaded without explicit user consent. Without proper consent mechanisms, any non-essential cookies or analytics tracking violates GDPR requirements for lawful basis.
findings.evidence: Login page (line 43) detects GPC signal but does not show an opt-in consent banner for analytics. CSP (line 278) allows plausible.io connections (analytics). Privacy policy mentions 'Technical and security data such as IP address, browser metadata, rate-limit events, and operational logs' but does not show a granular consent flow. No consent cookie or preference center visible in codebase.
findings.fix: Implement a cookie consent solution: (1) Add a consent banner on first load (non-intrusive but visible) that allows users to: accept essential cookies only, or accept all including analytics/marketing. (2) Store consent choices in localStorage and HttpOnly cookie with a 12-month expiry. (3) Only load plausible.io analytics script if user consents. (4) Create a preference center (linked from footer) allowing users to change consent at any time. (5) Document in privacy policy what cookies are used and for what purpose. (6) Implement consent records in the database (which user consented to what, when, which privacy policy version).

findings.title: Operational log retention policy not implemented or documented
findings.description: The privacy policy states 'Operational logs are generally retained for about 30 days unless needed longer for security review' but there is no evidence of implemented data deletion or TTL mechanism in the codebase. DynamoDB table has a TTL attribute (expiresAt) but it appears to be used only for rate-limit entries and password reset codes, not for audit/operational logs. Without automated cleanup, logs could be retained indefinitely.
findings.evidence: DynamoDB TTL is configured for the 'expiresAt' attribute (line 38-41) but logging calls at line 295-301 use console.log and do not set an expiresAt value. CloudFront access logs (terraform/cloudfront.tf line 132-135) are logged to S3 with no expiration policy visible. AWS CloudWatch logs (likely used for Lambda) have no retention policy visible in Terraform.
findings.fix: (1) Add explicit TTL to all DynamoDB log entries: set expiresAt = current_time + 30 days when logging. (2) For S3 access logs, configure S3 Lifecycle Policy to delete objects after 30 days. (3) For CloudWatch Logs, set log group retention to 30 days. (4) For any application-level logging (e.g., to external service), implement rotation and deletion. (5) Implement a data retention policy document and review logs quarterly. (6) Ensure logging does not capture sensitive data (passwords, tokens, PII); if needed, hash or redact before logging.

findings.title: Logs may contain sensitive data without documented sanitization
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.15 & A.8.16 (Logging & Audit Trails) and A.8.25 (Input Validation)
findings.description: The logging function (logAuthEvent at line 295-301) logs to console without explicitly excluding sensitive fields. While passwords are hashed in the database, other sensitive information like email addresses, user IDs, and request metadata are logged. The password reset code is stored in plaintext in DynamoDB (line 755), and error messages in API responses may leak information about user existence (though email enumeration is partially protected).
findings.evidence: Line 295-301: logAuthEvent logs timestamps and event names with details object that may contain userId, emailHash, and reason. Line 609-610 and 690-691 hash the email before logging but still reveal existence of authentication attempts. Line 755 stores reset code in plaintext (should be hashed). No documented policy on what fields are safe to log.
findings.fix: (1) Define a logging policy: document which fields are safe to log (timestamps, event names, anonymized/hashed identifiers). (2) Implement log sanitization: before logging any field, check against a 'safe fields' whitelist. (3) Hash or redact PII before logging. (4) Hash password reset codes in DynamoDB (use timing-safe comparison on verification like passwords). (5) Test that logs do not contain passwords, tokens, or plaintext PII. (6) Document retention and access controls for logs (who can view, from where).

findings.title: Multi-factor authentication not implemented despite security claims
findings.description: The README and privacy policy describe the system as secure and trustworthy, but there is no MFA support. In a system handling event organizer data and attendee information (PII), MFA should be available for users with administrative access or handling sensitive event data. Current single-factor authentication (password + email verification) is insufficient for a B2B platform.
findings.evidence: Login handler (line 669-718) performs password verification and returns token without any MFA challenge. No routes for MFA setup, verification, or enforcement visible in the API routes (lines 425-520). Login page has no MFA input fields.
findings.fix: (1) Implement optional TOTP (Time-based One-Time Password) using libraries like speakeasy or similar. (2) Add API endpoints: POST /api/auth/mfa/setup (returns QR code), POST /api/auth/mfa/verify (validates TOTP), POST /api/auth/mfa/disable (requires current password). (3) Require MFA for admin accounts (ADMIN_EMAILS set at line 16-18). (4) Add MFA state to token (mfaVerified flag). (5) After login, if MFA is enabled, require TOTP verification before returning full access token. (6) Provide backup recovery codes for account access if authenticator app is lost.

findings.title: No Data Protection Impact Assessment documented for event data processing
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 25 (Data Protection by Design and Default) & Art. 35 (DPIA)
findings.description: The system processes personal data of event organizers, attendees, speakers, vendors, and booth managers. This includes names, emails, event attendance information, photos (gallery feature), and potentially sensitive role/access information. GDPR Art. 35 requires a DPIA where processing is likely to result in high risk. While the README mentions the app stores 'event and organizer data such as saved local notes, preview settings, bookmarks, and preferences,' there's no documented DPIA or risk assessment.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy lists data collected but does not mention DPIA or specific risk mitigations for event data. README (lines 43-72) describes various data collected but no mention of privacy impact assessment. No DPIA document visible in docs/.
findings.fix: (1) Conduct a DPIA covering: event photo gallery (collection of attendee photos), role-based access (tracking who has admin/organizer permissions), event attendance tracking, email marketing (event reminders), third-party data sharing (if any). (2) Document: data flow, purposes, retention, recipients, rights of data subjects, technical/organizational safeguards, risks, and mitigations. (3) Store DPIA in a protected document (not public) but be prepared to share with supervisory authorities if requested. (4) If high-risk processing is found, document consultation with DPO if applicable. (5) Review DPIA annually or when processing significantly changes.

findings.title: Backup and disaster recovery procedures not documented
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.13 (Backup & Recovery) and A.5.28 (Incident Response)
findings.description: While DynamoDB has point-in-time recovery enabled (terraform/dynamodb.tf line 43-45), there is no documented backup strategy, recovery time objectives (RTO), recovery point objectives (RPO), or disaster recovery runbook. No mention of backup testing or verification procedures. Incident response procedures are not documented in the codebase.
findings.evidence: DynamoDB has PITR enabled but no S3 backup, DynamoDB Exports, or multi-region replication visible. README and CLAUDE.md do not mention backup or disaster recovery procedures. No runbook for incident response (breach, service outage, data corruption) visible.
findings.fix: (1) Document RTO and RPO: define acceptable downtime and data loss (e.g., RTO 4 hours, RPO 1 hour). (2) Enable DynamoDB Exports to S3 on a daily schedule (via Lambda + CloudWatch Events). (3) Implement S3 cross-region replication for critical backups. (4) Document backup retention: keep 7 days of daily exports, 4 weeks of weekly exports. (5) Test restore procedures quarterly and document results. (6) Create incident response runbook: breach (immediate steps, notification, investigation), data loss (restore from backup), service outage (failover plan). (7) Assign incident commander and escalation contacts.

findings.title: Admin functionality not restricted by role-based access control
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.5 (Access Control) and A.5.9 (Principle of Least Privilege)
findings.description: Admin access is determined by checking email membership in a hardcoded ADMIN_EMAILS set (lambda/api/index.mjs line 16-18) containing only 'cody@codyjo.com'. This is not scalable and lacks audit trail. There is no granular role-based access control (RBAC) system. As the system grows and additional staff need access, admin privileges cannot be delegated or revoked via UI.
findings.evidence: Line 16-18: ADMIN_EMAILS hardcoded set with only one email. Line 219-225: isAdmin and requireAdmin functions check membership. No UI or database-driven role assignment visible. No audit log of admin actions.
findings.fix: (1) Create a ROLES table: id, userId, role (admin, organizer, viewer, etc.), grantedAt, grantedBy, revokedAt. (2) Replace hardcoded ADMIN_EMAILS with database query: queryCommand for USER#X/ROLE. (3) Add admin UI: list users, grant/revoke roles, view audit log. (4) Log all role changes and admin actions: CREATE event with adminUserId, targetUserId, action, before/after state. (5) Implement role-based permission checks for all sensitive endpoints. (6) Document roles and their capabilities. (7) Require re-authentication for sensitive admin operations (password prompt).

findings.title: JWT secret rotation policy not documented
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.24 (Key Management) and A.5.12 (Access Control)
findings.description: JWT secret is stored in AWS Secrets Manager (lambda/api/index.mjs line 25-36) and cached after cold start. There is no documented rotation policy. If the secret is compromised, old tokens remain valid until expiry (30 days per line 178). Lack of key rotation guidance increases risk of long-term compromise.
findings.evidence: getJwtSecret() caches the secret after first retrieval (line 24). Token expiry is hardcoded to 30 days (line 178: exp: now + 86400 * 30). No key rotation Lambda or procedure visible.
findings.fix: (1) Document JWT secret rotation policy: rotate every 90 days (or immediately if compromise suspected). (2) Implement key versioning in Secrets Manager: store both current and previous secrets, try current first, fall back to previous if verification fails. (3) Create a rotation Lambda triggered by EventBridge on a 90-day schedule. (4) Shorter token expiry: consider 1-7 days instead of 30 days, requiring refresh tokens for longer sessions. (5) Implement token revocation list (denylist) for compromised tokens or explicit logout. (6) Document rotation procedure and test it in a dev environment.

findings.title: Privacy information not provided for event attendees registered by organizers
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 14 (Information to be provided where personal data are not obtained from the data subject)
findings.description: The system allows event organizers to import or register attendees (guest list, booth manager endpoints visible). When an attendee is registered by an organizer (not self-registration), the attendee may not receive privacy information about how their data is processed. GDPR Art. 14 requires disclosure of controller identity, purposes, recipients, rights, etc. when data is collected indirectly.
findings.evidence: API routes at line 434-435 show /api/events/[slug]/register and /api/events/[slug]/self-checkin endpoints. Guest list and booth manager features allow organizers to add attendees. No evidence of automatic privacy notice being sent to attendees added by organizers.
findings.fix: (1) When an organizer adds an attendee to an event (guest list, booth), send an email to the attendee with: controller identity (Cordivent), purposes (event coordination, attendance tracking), retention period, rights (access, deletion, portability). (2) Include a link to the full privacy policy and unsubscribe link. (3) Log that notification was sent. (4) Provide organizers with a template/guidance on what privacy info to include when manually inviting attendees. (5) In-app notification option for attendees already in the system: notify them that they were added to [event name] and link to privacy info.

findings.title: Unclear lawful basis for processing reading interpretations and analytics
findings.description: While registration requires consent to the privacy policy, the privacy policy does not clearly articulate the specific lawful basis for each processing activity. The policy mentions sending reading context to Anthropic (Claude API) as 'automated decision-making support you explicitly request', but does not establish a clear legal basis (consent, contract, legitimate interest, etc.) for collecting and processing analytics data (IP address, browser metadata, rate-limit events). Art. 6 requires a lawful basis for every processing activity.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy states 'Why we use it' includes 'To generate readings and operate the features you explicitly request', but does not distinguish between processing bases for different activities. Analytics collection (IP, browser metadata) lacks explicit basis disclosure.
