findings.fix: Revise the privacy policy to explicitly state the lawful basis for each major processing activity: (1) Account data and readings: Contract (Art. 6(1)(b)) as necessary for service provision; (2) Analytics: Legitimate interest (Art. 6(1)(f)) - specify the legitimate interests being served; (3) Email communications: Consent for marketing, contract for transactional. Create a detailed processing activity table or data mapping document. Ensure GDPR Art. 13 transparency for all collection points.

findings.title: No age verification or parental consent mechanism for users under 16
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 8, COPPA (15 U.S.C. § 6501-6506), California AADC (AB 2273)
findings.description: The privacy policy explicitly states 'intended only for people who are at least 16 years old' and 'not directed to children under 13', but there is no age gate, age verification, or parental consent mechanism at registration. A user can register with any age and the system does not verify or restrict access. Under GDPR Art. 8, processing of children's data under 16 requires parental consent (in most EU member states). Under COPPA (for US users), children under 13 require verifiable parental consent. Under California's AADC (AB 2273), businesses likely accessed by children must implement age-appropriate design and protections. The absence of enforcement allows minors to create accounts and have their data processed without proper safeguards.
findings.evidence: handleRegister function accepts registration from any user without age verification. No age gate UI component exists. Privacy policy (src/app/privacy/page.tsx:99-100) states age requirement in text form only.
findings.fix: Implement one of the following: (1) Age gate at registration requiring DOB entry and verification (reject users under 16); (2) Parental consent flow for users 13-16 (requires email verification from parent); (3) Age estimation using third-party service (Yoti, AgeChecker, etc.). For US users, implement COPPA compliance: age verification, parental consent for <13, verifiable parental email. Document compliance in privacy policy and DPIA. Consider geographic detection to apply jurisdiction-specific rules. Add prominent age disclaimer on login/registration pages.

findings.title: No documented Data Processing Agreements with third-party processors
findings.description: The privacy policy discloses that reading context is sent to Anthropic (Claude API) for interpretation and that AWS and Resend are used for hosting and email. However, there is no evidence of documented Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) in place with these vendors. Under GDPR Art. 28, any processor who handles personal data on behalf of a controller must have a written DPA in place that specifies: processor obligations, instructions for data processing, confidentiality, security measures, data subject rights support, and subprocessor authorization. The absence of DPAs is a direct violation of GDPR.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy states: 'When you request an AI interpretation or related AI feature, the relevant reading context may be processed by Anthropic to return that response. The app is hosted primarily on Amazon Web Services in the United States, and transactional emails may use Resend where configured.' No DPA links or references present in code or README.
findings.fix: Obtain or review DPAs from: (1) Anthropic for Claude API usage; (2) AWS for S3, Lambda, DynamoDB, CloudFront, Secrets Manager services; (3) Resend for email service. Ensure DPAs include: data subject request support, subprocessor notification, deletion/return of data on termination, audit rights, data security obligations. Create a Data Processing Register documenting all processors. Link DPA summaries in the privacy policy or provide on request.

findings.title: Incomplete disclosure of international data transfers and adequacy decisions
findings.description: The privacy policy states 'The app is hosted primarily on Amazon Web Services in the United States' but does not explicitly inform EU users that their data is transferred outside the EEA. GDPR Art. 13 requires controllers to inform data subjects of transfers outside the EEA and the legal mechanism ensuring adequate protection (e.g., Standard Contractual Clauses, adequacy decision, Binding Corporate Rules). While the US is not designated as having adequate protection under GDPR, transfers can proceed under SCCs or other mechanisms. The privacy policy must inform users of this transfer and the safeguards in place.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy states 'The app is hosted primarily on Amazon Web Services in the United States' but does not explain: (1) legal mechanism for transfer (SCCs, adequacy decision, etc.); (2) that EU users' data is stored and processed in the US; (3) Schrems II implications; (4) right to supplementary measures.
findings.fix: Revise privacy policy to include: (1) Explicit statement that personal data is transferred to and processed in the United States by AWS, Anthropic, and Resend; (2) Statement that the US is not deemed to have adequate data protection by the European Commission; (3) Explanation of safeguards: Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) in place with US processors, compliance with Schrems II supplementary measures where applicable; (4) Link to Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) or summary; (5) EU users' rights to request supplementary safeguards; (6) Contact information for data transfer inquiries. Ensure SCCs are in place with all US processors.

findings.title: Missing explicit API rate limiting on resource-intensive endpoints
findings.description: While rate limiting is implemented for authentication endpoints (registration: 3/hour, login: 5/15min, account deletion: 5/15min), there is no evidence of rate limiting on resource-intensive API endpoints like POST /api/interpret (AI interpretation) and POST /api/readings/share (public share generation). These endpoints could be abused to: (1) Generate high numbers of API calls to Anthropic (cost and DoS); (2) Create numerous share tokens; (3) Exceed DynamoDB capacity. Without rate limiting, a malicious user can cause service degradation or unexpected costs.
findings.evidence: Rate limiting is implemented for REGISTER, LOGIN, and account deletion via checkRateLimit() function, but handleShareReading (line 296), handleSaveReading, and other endpoints lack explicit rate limiting. No rate limiting logic found in index.mjs outside of auth handlers.
findings.fix: Implement rate limiting on POST endpoints: (1) POST /api/interpret: limit to 10 interpretations per user per hour; (2) POST /api/readings: limit to 20 readings per user per day; (3) POST /api/readings/share: limit to 5 shares per user per hour; (4) POST /api/journals: limit to 10 journals per user per day. Use the existing checkRateLimit() function pattern. Log rate limit events for monitoring. Consider using x-ratelimit headers in responses to inform clients of limits.

findings.title: Incomplete audit logging and missing security event coverage
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.15, A.8.16 (Logging and Audit Trails)
findings.description: While the application logs some security events (auth_register_duplicate, data_export, auth_account_delete, auth_password_reset), audit logging is not comprehensive. Missing audit coverage includes: (1) Successful logins and login failures (only failed registrations logged); (2) Email changes and password changes (code has logging for delete, but grep confirms no logging for changePassword/changeEmail endpoints); (3) Journal access and reads; (4) Admin API access and actions; (5) Data modification events (reading creation, updates); (6) Encryption key generation/recovery. Audit trails must be immutable, timestamped, and retention policies defined.
findings.evidence: Code shows console.log for specific events: 'auth_account_delete' (line 299), 'data_export', but missing logs for: successful logins, email changes, password changes (other than deleteAccount), admin actions, reading creation/modification.
findings.fix: Expand audit logging to cover all security-relevant events: (1) Authentication: successful login, failed login, password change, email change, account deletion; (2) Authorization: failed access attempts to protected endpoints, admin actions; (3) Data access: journal reads (consider if necessary), export data requests; (4) Encryption: key generation, key recovery attempts, key rotation; (5) Third-party integrations: AI interpretation requests sent to Anthropic; (6) Sensitive operations: account modifications, reading sharing. Log: event type, user ID, timestamp, IP address, result (success/failure). Do not log passwords, tokens, or sensitive data. Implement log retention policy (e.g., 90 days for operational logs, 1 year for security logs). Consider shipping logs to CloudWatch Logs with long-term S3 archival for immutability.

findings.title: Vague data retention policy for operational logs
findings.description: The privacy policy states 'Operational logs are generally retained for about 30 days unless needed longer for security review' (README.md: line 89). This is vague and does not meet GDPR transparency requirements. GDPR Art. 5(1)(e) requires that personal data be kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than necessary. The policy should specify: (1) exact retention periods for different data categories; (2) how retention is enforced (TTL, scheduled cleanup, manual deletion); (3) when 'longer retention for security review' is triggered; (4) what happens after retention period expires.
findings.evidence: README states: 'Operational logs are generally retained for about 30 days unless needed longer for security review.' No explicit retention schedule, TTL configuration, or cleanup job found in code. CloudWatch Logs retention not configured in Terraform.
findings.fix: Establish and document explicit retention schedules: (1) Operational logs (auth, API errors): 30 days auto-expiry via CloudWatch Logs retention; (2) Security-relevant logs (failures, deletions, exports): 90 days; (3) Billing/audit logs: 7 years (for regulatory/tax purposes); (4) User data (readings, journals): indefinite until user deletion. Implement retention via CloudWatch Logs retention policies, DynamoDB TTL, and S3 lifecycle policies. Document in privacy policy and operational runbooks. Update Terraform to enforce retention.

findings.title: Potential exposure of sensitive data in logs
findings.description: While the application is careful not to log full tokens or passwords, the current logging approach logs user IDs and IP addresses for audit purposes. If logs are exposed or accessed by unauthorized parties, these can be correlated to identify users. Additionally, CloudWatch Logs and application error responses may expose sensitive information: (1) Error messages may leak internal API structure or backend details; (2) Exception stack traces in development might expose code paths; (3) CloudWatch Logs are accessible to IAM-permitted users without end-to-end encryption.
findings.evidence: Logging statements include userId and IP (line 299: 'userId: user.userId, ip: event.requestContext?.http?.sourceIp'). Error response (line 299) returns generic 'Internal server error' (good), but detailed error logs go to CloudWatch. No mention of log encryption or access controls in Terraform.
findings.fix: Implement comprehensive log handling: (1) Mask or hash sensitive data in logs: user IDs can be hashed, IP addresses can be anonymized (truncate last octet); (2) Use structured logging (JSON) for easier parsing and filtering; (3) Implement log access controls: restrict CloudWatch Logs access to authorized personnel via IAM; (4) Enable CloudWatch Logs encryption at rest (already available in AWS); (5) Set log retention for CloudWatch Logs to 30-90 days; (6) Never log request/response bodies containing sensitive data; (7) Use AWS VPC endpoints to route logs securely; (8) Document log access and audit logs.

findings.title: No age-appropriate design considerations for minor users
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 8, COPPA (Children's Online Privacy Protection Act)
findings.description: While the app is 'not directed to children', if minors access the app, there are no design safeguards to protect their interests. GDPR Art. 8 and California AADC (AB 2273) require age-appropriate design for services likely to be used by children. This includes: (1) Default high-privacy settings for minors; (2) No dark patterns (infinite scroll, autoplay, addictive design); (3) Prominent, accessible privacy information in language suitable for children; (4) No profiling or behavioral targeting of minors; (5) Restrictions on data sharing with third parties. The app has no age-specific safeguards.
findings.evidence: No age detection logic in code. Settings page treats all users equally (no 'minor mode'). UI design does not include age-specific protections. No parental controls or guardian approval flows.
findings.fix: If the app is intentionally not targeting minors, consider: (1) Adding prominent age gate and terms confirming 16+ access only; (2) If minor access is anticipated, implement age-appropriate mode: high-privacy defaults, disable data sharing, no behavioral tracking, simplified UI with large text, accessible privacy information; (3) Parental dashboard for minors 13-16 (parental approval required); (4) Restrict features available to minors (e.g., no public sharing, limits on data export); (5) No marketing or promotional content to minors. Document compliance in privacy policy and DPIA.

findings.title: Incomplete documentation of backup and disaster recovery procedures
findings.description: The DynamoDB configuration includes point-in-time recovery (PITR) enabled, which is excellent. However, there is no documented backup strategy, recovery time objective (RTO), recovery point objective (RPO), or tested recovery procedures. ISO 27001 A.8.13 requires documented backup and recovery procedures with regular testing.
findings.evidence: DynamoDB PITR enabled (line 38-40), but no backup strategy, RTO/RPO, or disaster recovery documentation found in code comments, README, or ops documentation. No backup testing mentioned.
findings.fix: Document and implement: (1) Backup strategy: DynamoDB PITR (current), daily full backups to S3, cross-region replication for failover; (2) Define RTO and RPO: RTO <4 hours, RPO <1 hour; (3) Recovery procedures: documented runbooks for DynamoDB recovery, S3 data recovery, Lambda redeployment; (4) Test recovery quarterly: execute full restore from backup to test environment; (5) Communicate backup and recovery procedures to ops team; (6) Monitor backup success via CloudWatch alarms; (7) Document in runbooks and architecture documentation.

findings.title: CSP allows unsafe-inline scripts (acceptable but verbose)
findings.description: The Content Security Policy (CSP) in Terraform (cloudfront.tf, line 235) includes 'script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' https://plausible.io'. While 'unsafe-inline' is necessary for Plausible analytics initialization and is from a trusted third party, it reduces the effectiveness of CSP against XSS attacks. Additionally, the CSP allows inline styles ('style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline''), which could be exploited. This is a minor issue but represents a defense-in-depth opportunity.
findings.evidence: CSP policy includes: "script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' https://plausible.io" and "style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'". While inline scripts are minimized in the codebase, this relaxation of CSP weakens XSS protection.
findings.fix: Consider: (1) Moving Plausible initialization to an external script file to remove need for 'unsafe-inline'; (2) Using Content Security Policy nonces for any unavoidable inline scripts; (3) Restricting style-src to 'self' only and using CSS classes instead of inline styles; (4) Adding script-src hash if Plausible init code is fixed. For now, 'unsafe-inline' is acceptable given the trusted nature of Plausible, but evaluate in future security reviews.
